title: Low-Depth Algebraic Circuit Lower Bounds Over Any Field
speaker: Michael A. Forbes
abstract: The recent breakthrough of Limaye, Srinivasan and Tavenas (LST) gave the first super-polynomial lower bounds against low-depth algebraic circuits, for any field of zero (or sufficiently large) characteristic. It was an open question to extend this result to small-characteristic, which in particular is relevant for an approach to prove superpolynomial AC⁰[p]-Frege lower bounds. In this work, we prove super-polynomial algebraic circuit lower bounds against low-depth algebraic circuits over any field, with the same parameters as LST. We give two proofs. The first is logical, showing that even though the proof of LST naively fails in small characteristic, the proof is sufficiently algebraic that generic transfer results imply the result over characteristic zero implies the result over all fields. Motivated by this indirect proof, we then proceed to give a second constructive proof, replacing the field-dependent set-multilinearization result of LST with a set-multilinearization that works over any field, by using the Binet-Minc identity.

proof_stages.label: arbitrary-field low-depth lower-bound goal
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates small-characteristic algebraic circuit lower bounds via PIT, Newton identities, and the LST breakthrough, then states the target theorem: an explicit degree-about-log-n polynomial has quasipolynomial depth-three lower bounds over every field.

proof_stages.label: LST reduction template and characteristic bottleneck
proof_stages.summary: The LST proof is organized as depth-three to homogeneous depth-five, then to set-multilinear depth-five, then a set-multilinear lower bound, with only the homogenization step requiring large characteristic.

proof_stages.label: syntactic restrictions and Newton-identity obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Homogeneity and set-multilinearity are defined, LST's low-depth homogenization is explained through elementary symmetric polynomials and Newton identities, and the failure of Newton-like identities in small characteristic is identified as the main obstruction.

proof_stages.label: integer polynomial identity transfer principle
proof_stages.summary: The first proof begins with the elementary observation that a zero integer polynomial remains zero after interpretation over any field, suggesting that a characteristic-zero proof can be transferred once its assertions are encoded as polynomial identities.

proof_stages.label: rank-method encoding of lower bounds
proof_stages.summary: LST's lower-bound method is reformulated in terms of matrices whose entries are integer linear combinations of polynomial coefficients, so low rank becomes vanishing of minors and can be transferred across characteristic, rational-function extensions, and specializations.

proof_stages.label: universal-circuit characteristic-transfer proof
proof_stages.summary: An integer-coefficient universal depth-three circuit is viewed over a characteristic-zero rational-function field, LST gives its associated matrix low rank, and the rank-transfer lemmas show that every small depth-three circuit over any field also has low rank, contradicting LST's large-rank explicit polynomial.

proof_stages.label: constructive direct set-multilinearization via Binet-Minc
proof_stages.summary: The second proof bypasses separate low-depth homogenization by going directly from arbitrary depth-three circuits to set-multilinear depth-five circuits over any field, using Binet-Minc circuits for rectangular permanents and their completeness for set-multilinearization.

examples.name: determinant multiplicativity identity
examples.note: The identity det(X)det(Y)=det(XY) illustrates how proving an integer polynomial identity in characteristic zero automatically proves it over every field.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the integer-polynomial transfer lemma to the determinant multiplicativity identity to explain how a characteristic-zero proof yields the identity over any field

examples.note: A univariate quadratic has a repeated root exactly when a small coefficient matrix has rank one, making the abstract rank-method paradigm concrete.

examples.name: Hadamard matrix rank in different characteristics
examples.note: In Q&A, Hadamard matrices illustrate that rank, and hence rank-based lower-bound behavior, can genuinely depend on the characteristic of the field.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only states that a Hadamard matrix has full rank in characteristic zero and logarithmic rank in characteristic two without showing why

passes.reason: The highly regular determinant-maximal Hadamard matrix has rank collapse in characteristic two, refuting characteristic-independent rank behavior.
